one.zh: PARENTING – Jurisdiction – Child is an Australian citizen – Child has been living in the Philippines since 2023 – Child no longer habitually resident …

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Jurisdiction – Child is an Australian citizen – Child has been living in the Philippines since 2023 – Child no longer habitually resident in Australia – Where there are ongoing proceedings in the Philippines – Court prevented from exercising jurisdiction by ss 111CC and 111CD of the Family Law Act – Application for parenting orders dismissed.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

RIETHMULLER J:

The applicant father applied for parenting orders on 21 August 2025 with respect to the parties’ six-year-old child born in 2020. The child, who is an Australian citizen, is presently living in the Philippines with the mother’s sister. The child does not yet have Philippines citizenship, although it appears would be entitled to citizenship once the formal application processes are completed. The mother’s sister has been joined (as the second respondent) to the proceedings and has been served but has not appeared or participated in the proceedings.
The father has previously brought proceedings in the Philippines with respect to contact with the child. He now seeks orders in Australia as both he and the mother are living here.
The mother argues that this court is prevented from exercising jurisdiction with respect to parenting issues as a result of Part XIIIAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) on the basis that there is a “competent authority’ in the Philippines and that s 111CD(1)(e) only permits this court to exercise its jurisdiction over the child (s 69E) if the child is “habitually resident” in Australia.
This court has jurisdiction pursuant to s 69E to make parenting orders with respect to children who are Australian citizens. However, the jurisdiction of this court is restricted by Division 4 of Part XIIIAA of the Act. The Division has effect despite the other provisions of the Act (see s 111CB).
In this case, there is no question that the Philippines has a “competent authority”, that is, it has functioning courts that may deal with children’s issues, as is apparent from the fact that proceedings are pending. It is therefore a country with a “competent authority” within the meaning of s 111CC which has exercised jurisdiction.
I must then turn to s 111CD to determine whether this court may exercise jurisdiction to make orders concerning the child. There are a number of factors that are relevant to this determination:
(a) First, there is no question that in this case, currently the child is not present in Australia.

(b) Second, the child is not present in a Convention country, in that the Philippines is not a signatory to the Convention on Jurisdiction, Applicable Law, Recognition, Enforcement and Cooperation in respect of Parental Responsibility and Measures for the Protection of Children signed at The Hague on 19 October 1996 (the “Convention”).

(c) Third, the Philippines has a competent authority for dealing with children’s issues, which appears to be the Regional Trial Court. The court in the Philippines has exercised jurisdiction and has pending proceedings before it, and thus an issue as to jurisdiction has arisen.

The only relevant subparagraph of s 111CD that appears to apply in this case is subparagraph (1)(e), which covers the situation of a child who is present in a non-Convention country.
(1) A court may exercise jurisdiction for a Commonwealth personal protection measure only in relation to:
...

(e) a child who is present in a non-Convention country, if:
(i) the child is habitually resident in Australia; and

(ii) any of paragraphs 69E(1)(b) to (e) applies to the child...
Whether the child’s “habitual residence” is in Australia is a question of fact. In LK v Director‑General, Department of Community Services [2009] HCA 9; (2009) 237 CLR 582 (“LK”) the High Court identified that:
... First, application of the expression “habitual residence” permits consideration of a wide variety of circumstances that bear upon where a person is said to reside and whether that residence is to be described as habitual. Secondly, the past and present intentions of the person under consideration will often bear upon the significance that is to be attached to particular circumstances like the duration of a person’s connections with a particular place of residence.
The High Court went on to identify that “it may be accepted that ‘[h]abitual residence, consistent with the purpose of its use, identifies the center of a person’s personal and family life as disclosed by the facts of the individual’s activities’” and thus, a person could be habitually resident in more than one place or may have no place of habitual residence (at [25], as per the original).
Importantly, intention is not given “controlling weight” (at [28]), rather:
When speaking of the habitual residence of a child it will usually be very important to examine where the person or persons who are caring for the child live – where those persons have their habitual residence. The younger the child, the less sensible it is to speak of the place of habitual residence of the child as distinct from the place of habitual residence of the person or persons upon whom the child is immediately dependent for care and housing. But if, as the writings about the Abduction Convention and like instruments repeatedly urge, the question of habitual residence of a child is one of fact, it is important not to elevate the observation that a child looks to others for care and housing to some principle of law like the (former) law of dependent domicile of a married woman.
At [29]–[34] the High Court notes three important points about evidence of intention:
First, individuals do not always act with a clearly formed and singular view of what it is intended (or hoped) that the future will hold...
...
Secondly, because a person’s intentions may be ambiguous, in asking whether a person has abandoned residence in a place it is necessary to recognise the possibility that the person may not have formed a singular and irrevocable intention not to return, yet properly be described as no longer habitually resident in that place...
Thirdly, when considering where a child is habitually resident, attention cannot be confined to the intentions of the parent who in fact has the day‑to‑day care of the child. It will usually be necessary to consider what each parent intends for the child. When parents are living together, young children will have the same habitual residence as their parents. No less importantly, it may be accepted that the general rule is that neither parent can unilaterally change that place of habitual residence. The assent of the other parent (or a court order) would be necessary. But again, if it becomes necessary to examine the intentions of the parents, the possibility of ambiguity or uncertainty on the part of one or both of them must be acknowledged.
(Emphasis in original)
The High Court also explained that the apparent distinctions between various authorities “may not be great” when considering “whether presence at a place has a ‘degree of settled purpose from the child’s perspective’” (at [45], emphasis in original).
In State Central Authority & Metin [2020] FamCA 535 at [172] (“Metin”) Bennett J helpfully summarised the law, saying:
Most respectfully, in my view is that the above authorities can be reconciled as permitting the following propositions in relation to one parent be able to unilaterally change the habitual residence of a child:
a) First, one parent cannot, by intention alone, unilaterally alter a child’s habitual residence. It is not a matter where wishing will make it so;

b) Second, the most important inquiry is around the child’s actual experience and perspective. Parental intention may be relevant but will not prevail over the child’s life experience and outlook;

c) Third, any change of habitual residence will necessarily be based, in no small part, on the child’s integration into the new contracting state towards a point at which the child’s residence in that place can be characterised as habitual;

d) Fourth, one parent can act unilaterally of the other parent to bring about a change the habitual residence of a child by facilitating the child’s integration and assimilation into the new environment. It is, however, the child’s assimilation, rather than the taking parent’s intention or mindset, which drives the change in habitual residence;

e) Fifth, the younger the child and the more relevant parental intention may be given that a young child has a limited capacity to integrate into a community. That does not leave a young child without a habitual residence but it will likely bring other circumstances to the fore;

f) Sixth, the fact that one parent neither sanctions nor wants a child’s move to or retention in another country will not prevent the child from becoming habitually resident in the state to which the child is taken or in which the child is retained where the change in habitual residence can be demonstrated to be based on the child’s lived experience of being integrated to some degree into the new community and any age appropriate reorientation of the child’s perspective on where the child belongs.

The father referred to a number of authorities from overseas in his material which appear to have been generated by an AI engine. They can be dealt with briefly:
(a) De L v Director-General, NSW Department of Community Services (1996) 187 CLR 640 did not address the meaning of “habitual residence”.

(b) The propositions said to be stated in A v A (Children: Habitual Residence) [2013] UKSC 60 and Re B (A Child) (Habitual Residence) [2016] UKSC 4 are not rules that prevent establishing of habitual residence but relevant considerations.

(c) Mozes v Mozes [2001] USCA9 16; (2001) 239 F.3d 1067 is not authority for the proposition that “[h]abitual residence only changes when both parents intend to abandon the original residence.”, rather is authority that “[h]abitual residence is intended to be a description of a factual state of affairs, and a child can lose its habitual attachment to a place even without a parent’s consent. Even when there is no settled intent on the part of the parents to abandon the child’s prior habitual residence, courts should find a change in habitual residence if ‘the objective facts point unequivocally to a person’s ordinary or habitual residence being in a particular place.’ Zenel v. Haddow, 1993 S.L.T. 975, 979 (Scot. 1st Div.).” (at 1,081).

Counsel for the mother set out a number of propositions from the authorities in his Outline of Argument:
Determination of habitual residence is “a question of fact, based upon the individual considerations that the individual case throws forward.” (See Marley & Chadwick [2025] FedCFamC1F 922 at [71] citing Duckworth & Jamison [2014] FamCA 308 at [31]; Sterling & Sterling [2022] FedCFamC1A 3; (2022) FLC 94-070 at [25]; Bajek & Bajek [2024] FedCFamC1F 466 at [41].)
Habitual residence is to be determined at the date of the hearing. (See Marley & Chadwick [2025] FedCFamC1F 922 at [71]; Bunyon & Lewis (No 3) [2013] FamCA 888; Sun & Long [2019] FamCA 3; Ahmad & Hadi [2020] FamCA 1041; Yaling & Tsen [2022] FedCFamC1F 347; Ishak & Koroma [2023] FedCFamC1F 272; Bajek & Bajek [2024] FedCFamC1F 466.)
It will be necessary to consider what each parent intends for the child. (See Department of Communities and Justice & Leoni [2020] FamCA 411 referring to LK v Director-General, Department of Community Services [2009] HCA 9; (2009) 237 CLR 582)
There is no closed set of criteria or any predetermined weighting between them to establish habitual residence (LK v Director-General, Department of Community Services [2009] HCA 9; (2009) 237 CLR 582 at [35]).
In order to find someone is habitually resident in a place they must generally have lived there for an “appreciable period.” (See Zotkiewicz & Commissioner of Police (No 2) [2011] FamCAFC 147; (2011) FLC 93-472 at [74]- [75])
However, the period of time is not determinative. (See Secretary, Department of Family and Community Services & Padwa [2016] FamCAFC 57; (2016) FLC 93-701 at 81,314)
In State Central Authority & Metin, [2020] FamCA 535 at [172] Bennett J held that in the context of a unilateral change of a child’s habitual residence by one parent:
(a) the most important inquiry is around the child’s actual experience and perspective. Parental intention may be relevant but will not prevail over the child’s life experience and outlook; and

(b) any change of habitual residence will necessarily be based, in no small part, on the child’s integration into the new contracting state towards a point at which the child’s residence in that place can be characterised as habitual.

The importance of the statement by Bennett J in Metin that the “most important enquiry is around the child’s actual experience and perspective” reflects the statement by the High Court in LK that habitual residence “identifies the center of a person’s personal and family life as disclosed by the facts of the individual’s activities” (at [25], as per the original). The role of parental intentions is difficult to weigh in this context as habitual residence is ultimately a matter of fact reflecting the “child’s actual experience and perspective” (Metin at [172(b)]) and not the intentions or desires of the parents. However, the younger the child the less likely the child is to have experiences and perspectives beyond their immediate carers whereas the older a child the more likely they will have a range of experiences and connections beyond their primary carer, parents and family.

EVIDENCE OF THE PARTIES

Both the father and mother gave evidence and were cross examined. Both presented as reasonable witnesses.
During the parties’ relationship, the child lived in both Australia and the Philippines.
The child was born in Australia and lived here with the parents for the early part of the child’s life. From April 2022 until July 2023 the child attended a daycare centre in the Sydney region and participated in family and community activities. The mother says that her sister Ms B was living with them and assisting with the child’s care. Ms B had to stay until after COVID but then returned to the Philippines.
The father relocated with the child to the Philippines in July 2023 to work in the Philippines for an Australian company. He says that the move was intended to be temporary and for six to 12 months. Initially the mother remained at the family home in Australia. Following the child’s move to the Philippines with the father, the child spent weekends with the father and most weeknights with the mother’s family, primarily with Mr C (the mother’s cousin) and Ms D (the mother’s sister), who both assisted with the child’s care. The child did spend some weeknights with the father as the two homes were in the same complex (although in different towers).
The mother later joined the father and the child in September 2023. They rented a different residence, and it seems Ms D lived with them, but initially Ms B did not.
From September 2023 the father says that they both cared for the child and the mother says that she was the primary carer. I prefer the mother’s evidence on this issue, particularly in light of the fact that the father was continuing in his full-time employment.
The father returned to Australia without the mother or child in August 2024 when his work arrangements ended. It was intended that the mother and child would return soon after and the father made housing arrangements in expectation of their return. He says they separated in January 2025 and says that they discussed the child’s return to Australia and agreed it would be in two to four months.
From May 2025 the father says that the child’s continued presence in the Philippines was without his consent.
The mother agrees that the father left the Philippines in August 2024 and says that they remained a couple. She says that in December 2024 the father came to the Philippines (which she described as “came home” in her affidavit filed 6 February 2026 at paragraph 10) for one month. The parties were in dispute about the father going to City E (a city in the Philippines) for five days in early January 2025, but they remained a couple and signed a lease for a townhouse in January 2025. The mother says they had another argument and agreed to separate on 26 January 2025, the day before the father was due to return to Australia. The father left the next day and the mother says that the father has provided no financial support since then.
Following separation, the mother obtained an alternative residence for her and the child. The mother was unable to find stable work in the Philippines and returned to Australia in April 2025, leaving the child in the care of Ms B and her family, and says that her sister Ms D visits often.
In July 2025, the father commenced proceedings at a Regional Trial Court, City F (a city in the Philippines) under case number Spec. Proc. No. .... Those proceedings are ongoing. It appears that they commenced as habeas corpus proceedings but are now amended to be visitation proceedings. There is no expert evidence as to the detail of the operation of the family law of the Philippines. Whilst a copy of a relevant Code was tendered I am not satisfied that I can make findings as to the nuance of the operation of the family law of the Philippines without expert evidence.
The child has remained in the Philippines since 2023 in the care of the mother’s family and more recently assisted by a nanny. The child has been attending school in the Philippines and will graduate from pre‑school or prep in a couple of months. The mother plans to go back to the Philippines for that graduation. The mother is equivocal about the child returning to Australia; it seems that this is primarily because of concerns relating to whether stable care arrangements can be established for the child’s care in Australia.
The father says that the child does not hold citizenship of the Philippines and the child’s visa expired over two years ago. Whilst there are issues with the child’s citizenship or visa status they appear to be bureaucratic issues rather than substantive issues. Aside from the relevance of the father not giving consent to the child remaining in the Philippines there is little about this factor that goes to habitual residence.
In the background are proceedings for a nullity order with respect to the marriage of the parties. The father’s case is that the mother’s birth certificate (albeit incorrectly) recorded her as male and therefore the marriage is not able to be recognised in the Philippines. The mother, for her part raised concerns that the father’s nullity application may cause her to lose her residency rights in Australia and thus require her to return to the Philippines; thus, she is reticent to arrange for the child to come to Australia. These arguments are entirely speculative and cannot be assessed or determined on the material before the court. However, I accept that the mother’s concerns are genuinely held and as a result she has no clear intention as to the future residence of the child.

Applicant’s request to provide further submissions

Following the hearing the father sought to provide further evidence. My associate wrote to the parties on 27 February 2026, inviting the mother and the second respondent to make any submissions in reply to the father’s request by 6 March 2026. No response has been received.
The father provided a letter from his lawyer in the Philippines saying:
On [...] July 2025, the case was filed with [...] Regional Trial Court, [City F], a family court. But, during the [...] August 2025 hearing before [the court], Presiding Judge [name redacted] intimated to the undersigned the difficulty and complexity of serving summons upon the person of [the mother] because she resides in Australia.

It must be noted that despite the amendments to the Rules of Court in the Philippines which allows for the service of summons through electronic means in ordinary civil cases, A.M. No. 03-04-04-SC dated April 22, 2003, also known as the Rule on Custody of Minors and Writ of Habeas Corpus in relation to Custody of Minors, requires the personal service of summons to grant the family court jurisdiction over the person of [the mother].

Considering the foregoing, SPEC. PROC. NO. [...] was amended into a simple child visitation suit, which will grant you visitation rights to your child...

Currently, the sheriff of [the court] is attempting to serve summons upon the person of [the second respondent] and the case is still in the initial phases.
The letter does not provide evidence that personal service on the mother in Australia is prohibited by Philippines law, nor that it is not reasonably possible for the mother to be personally served in Australia (or in the Philippines when the mother returns to visit the child).
In the absence of clear evidence, I do not accept that a foreign court that has jurisdiction with respect to parenting issues over a child present in the foreign country would be unable to exercise its jurisdiction if one parent is overseas. The evidence goes no further than showing that the convenience of service by electronic means is not available in a habeas corpus case in the Philippines. There is no evidence that the mother has been evading service nor evidence of any specific request that the mother accept service. The evidence does not show that the court in the Philippines is unable to exercise jurisdiction with respect to the residence of the child. Rather, it suggests that the mother must be served personally.

CONSIDERATION

I accept that the family had intended to move to the Philippines for a period, although it seems not permanently, when the father obtained work there in 2023. It appears that the family’s intention was for them to all return to Australia, but that intention was no longer jointly held after January 2025, when the parties separated. By this time the child had lived in the Philippines for one and a half years. It appears that the mother intends to continue to live in Australia. I am persuaded that the intention of the parties is to live in Australia (albeit separated and not together) and that it is the mother’s intention for the child’s half sibling to also reside in Australia. Since the mother returned in April 2025 the child has continued living with the mother’s family. She says her intent is to bring the child to Australia, but the time frame is less than clear. The father’s strong intent is to have the child return to Australia if possible.
From the child’s perspective his presence in the Philippines with the mother’s family must have a degree of settled purpose as his mother and father have come and gone from his daily life over the last two and a half years and before that, when living in Australia, Ms B was a significant carer for the child. Whether it is in the child’s best interests to reside with his aunt, mother or father is not the question that must be answered in determining the jurisdictional question that arises under s 111CD(1)(e) of the Act.
In Montu & Dinah [2025] FedCFamC1F 628 children who had lived in another country for two and a half years (following one parent unilaterally relocating them from Australia) were found to be habitually resident in that country. Similarly, in Echolls & Rinna [2024] FedCFamC1F 275, I found that a child who had been in another country for three years following a unilateral relocation was habitually resident in that country. The difficult weighing of parental intentions was undertaken in Anandi & Saini [2023] FedCFamC1F 334[1] where a child was a baby when leaving Australia to be cared for by maternal family members. At the time of the hearing the child was a toddler and had been attending a toddler school and immersed in the family life, religion, and culture of the maternal family. Rees J found that the child was no longer habitually resident in Australia.
These cases can be contrasted with Marley & Chadwick [2025] FedCFamC1F 922 where a teenager travelled to another country to attend boarding school. It seems apparent that few children would believe their boarding school is an habitual residence and rather that the boarding school is a place providing lodging; to learn during their schooling years.
When considered from the child’s perspective, the Philippines is the only place of residence the child is likely to remember as few children have memories earlier than three to four years of age. The choices made by both parents have meant that the child’s life has developed as a life in the Philippines. I am persuaded that the child’s habitual residence is not in Australia at present. It seems most likely that the child’s habitual residence is in the Philippines. If the parental intention is given sufficient weight as to counterbalance the practical circumstances that point to the child being habitually resident in the Philippines, it is still difficult to see that the child’s habitual residence could be in Australia after so much time has passed: at best the child may not have a place of habitual residence. If the child has no place of habitual residence, the provisions operate to prevent this court from exercising jurisdiction if the child is present in a foreign country that has an “appropriate authority”.
The effect of the legislation as enacted is to prevent this court from exercising jurisdiction over the child even though he is an Australian citizen and both his parents are present and living in Australia. The legislation, as enacted, prevents protection being provided to an Australian citizen child if they have lived in another country for long enough that they are no longer habitually resident in Australia, despite that country not even being a signatory to the 1996 Convention. The legislation does not allow for jurisdiction to be exercised in the present case even if it was found to be in the child’s best interests.

CONCLUSION

On the evidence before the court, Part XIIIAA prohibits this court from exercising jurisdiction with respect to the child at this time.
I must therefore dismiss the father’s application for parenting orders.
